label: Yovelim (Jubilees) — Part 2 — Chapters 26–50
summary_plain: Yovelim Part 2 continues the retelling from Yaakov to the departure from Egypt. Yaakov serves Lavan for Leah and Rachel. He returns to the land and is renamed Yisra'EL. Levi is ordained as priest of the Most High YHWH in a dream at Bethel. Yaakov's twelve sons are born with jubilee dates. Yosef is sold into Egypt. Yaakov goes down to Egypt with seventy souls — YHWH appears to him and says: I am the Creator of your father. The Yovelim text gives the full Pesach law: the lamb on the fourteenth of the first month, eaten by night on the fifteenth. The final chapter gives the Shabbat laws and the jubilee structure: the land shall rest for ten weeks of years. The angel of the presence declares: this entire history is written for you and the Sabbaths of the years.
summary_scholarly: Chapters 26 through 50 complete the Yovelim retelling — from Yaakov's sojourn with Lavan through the Pesach and the jubilee structure. The ACR reads from 4QJub-a through 4QJub-g and the Orit Ge'ez. The ordination of Levi in chapter 32 is significant: Levi dreams that he is ordained priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever. This establishes the Levitical priesthood before the Sinai legislation. The descent into Egypt (ch 45) includes YHWH's direct appearance to Yaakov: 'I am YHWH the Creator of your father.' The Pesach law in chapter 49 is the most detailed Pesach legislation outside the Torah itself — with specific times (kill before evening, eat by night on the fifteenth, from sunset) and warnings. Chapter 50 closes the book with the Shabbat laws and the jubilee framework, then the final command: everything is written. The angel of the presence confirms: the entire chronological structure is authored from the heavenly tablets.

key_terms.definition: In chapter 32, Levi dreams at Bethel that he is ordained priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever. This establishes the priesthood before the Torah is given at Sinai.

Chapter 49 gives the most detailed Pesach instructions: kill the lamb on the fourteenth of the first month before evening. Eat it by night on the fifteenth from the time of sunset. Every detail is dated and commanded.
Yaakov goes down to Egypt with seventy souls (ch 45), matching Bereshit and Shemot. YHWH appears to him at Beersheva before the descent.
Chapter 50 gives specific Shabbat commandments — what may and may not be done on the seventh day. The Shabbat is tied to the jubilee structure and the heavenly order.
The entire book ends by affirming the jubilee framework: 'the Sabbaths of the years... the jubilees... till the land shall rest' (50:1). History is measured in jubilee periods from creation to Sinai.
In chapter 32, Yaakov tithes all that came with him. He counts his sons and sets apart Levi as the tenth — the tithe — to YHWH. This is the reason Levi becomes the priestly tribe.
Chapter 30 records the law against intermarriage as written on the heavenly tablets. The prohibition is tied to the holiness of Yisra'EL as YHWH's people.

YHWH appears to Yaakov at Beersheva before the descent to Egypt: 'I am YHWH the Creator of your father — I am the Creator of Avraham and the Creator of Yitzhak' (45:2-4). A direct theophany confirming the descent is within the plan.
The book ends with the angel of the presence confirming that everything has been written from the heavenly tablets and commanded to Moshe. The entire text is framed as heavenly revelation.
The land shall rest for 'ten weeks of years' (50:1) — the jubilee cycles that structure the future beyond Sinai.

fill_blank.prompt: I am YHWH the Creator of your father — I am the Creator of ______ and the Creator of Yitzhak.
fill_blank.source_quote: And he said: I am YHWH the Creator of your father — I am the Creator of Avraham and the Creator of Yitzhak.

fill_blank.prompt: Remember the commandment which YHWH commanded you concerning the ______, that you should celebrate it in its season on the fourteenth of the first month.
fill_blank.source_quote: Remember the commandment which YHWH commanded you concerning the Passover, that you should celebrate it in its season on the fourteenth of the first month.

fill_blank.prompt: Levi dreamed that they had ordained and made him the ______ of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever.
fill_blank.source_quote: And he dwelt that night at Bethel, and Levi dreamed that they had ordained and made him the priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever.

fill_blank.prompt: And after this law I gave you the command of the ______ of the land.
fill_blank.source_quote: And after this law I gave you the command of the Sabbaths of the land, and the jubilees in the years of weeks.

fill_blank.prompt: He served him for Rachel his daughter one ______ of years.
fill_blank.source_quote: And in the second year of this week Yaakov came to the land of the east, to Lavan the brother of Rivkah his mother, and he was with him, and he served him for Rachel his daughter one week of years.

fill_blank.prompt: And this entire history I have ______ for you.
fill_blank.source_quote: And this entire history I have written for you and the Sabbaths of the years I have reported to you.

fill_blank.prompt: That you should kill it before it is evening, and that they should eat it by night on the evening of the ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: That you should kill it before it is evening, and that they should eat it by night on the evening of the fifteenth from the time of the setting of the sun.

fill_blank.prompt: And Yisra'EL took his journey with all that he had, and came to ______, and offered sacrifices.
fill_blank.source_quote: And Yisra'EL took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheva, and offered sacrifices to the Creator of his father Yitzhak.

fill_blank.prompt: And Yaakov rose early in the morning, on the fourteenth of this month, and he gave a ______ of all that came with him.
fill_blank.source_quote: And Yaakov rose early in the morning, on the fourteenth of this month, and he gave a tithe of all that came with him.

fill_blank.prompt: And YHWH appeared to Yisra'EL in a vision of the ______ and said: Yaakov, Yaakov.
fill_blank.source_quote: And YHWH appeared to Yisra'EL in a vision of the night and said: Yaakov, Yaakov.

multiple_choice.question: What did Levi dream at Bethel?
options: That he was ordained priest of the Most High YHWH forever
That the temple would be built
That he would lead the army
multiple_choice.source_quote: Levi dreamed that they had ordained and made him the priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever.

multiple_choice.question: When is the Pesach lamb to be killed according to Yovelim?
options: The tenth of the first month
The fourteenth of the first month before evening
The first of the seventh month
The fifteenth of the first month at dawn
multiple_choice.source_quote: You should celebrate it in its season on the fourteenth of the first month — that you should kill it before it is evening.

multiple_choice.question: What did YHWH say to Yaakov at Beersheva before the descent to Egypt?
options: I am YHWH the Creator of your father — the Creator of Avraham and Yitzhak
multiple_choice.source_quote: I am YHWH the Creator of your father — I am the Creator of Avraham and the Creator of Yitzhak.

multiple_choice.question: What does chapter 50 give the command of?
options: The Sabbaths of the land and the jubilees
multiple_choice.source_quote: And after this law I gave you the command of the Sabbaths of the land, and the jubilees in the years of weeks.

multiple_choice.question: Why did Levi become the priestly tribe?
options: He was Yaakov's tithe — the tenth son set apart to YHWH
multiple_choice.source_quote: And Yaakov rose early in the morning, on the fourteenth of this month, and he gave a tithe of all that came with him.

multiple_choice.question: How long did Yaakov serve Lavan for Rachel?
multiple_choice.source_quote: He served him for Rachel his daughter one week of years.

multiple_choice.question: When is the Pesach lamb to be eaten?
options: By night on the evening of the fifteenth from sunset
multiple_choice.source_quote: They should eat it by night on the evening of the fifteenth from the time of the setting of the sun.

multiple_choice.question: Who confirms that the entire history has been written?
multiple_choice.source_quote: And this entire history I have written for you and the Sabbaths of the years I have reported to you.

multiple_choice.question: Where did Yaakov offer sacrifices before going down to Egypt?
multiple_choice.source_quote: And Yisra'EL took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheva, and offered sacrifices to the Creator of his father Yitzhak.

multiple_choice.question: What does chapter 30 record as written on the heavenly tablets?
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Law Against Intermarriage — The Heavenly Tablets Record.

faq.question: Why is Levi's ordination important in Yovelim?
faq.answer: In chapter 32, Levi dreams at Bethel that he is ordained priest of the Most High YHWH — him and his sons forever. Yaakov counts his sons and Levi is the tenth — the tithe. This establishes the Levitical priesthood BEFORE the Torah is given at Sinai. The priesthood is not a later invention; it is written on the heavenly tablets and confirmed in a vision at Bethel.

faq.question: What does the Yovelim Pesach law add to the Torah?
faq.answer: Chapter 49 gives the most detailed Pesach legislation: kill the lamb on the fourteenth of the first month before evening, eat it by night on the fifteenth from sunset, with specific warnings about timing. The Yovelim instructions complement Shemot 12 and Vayikra 23 — they are not separate from the Torah but are an expansion of the same law, dictated from the heavenly tablets.

faq.question: How does Yovelim end?
faq.answer: Chapter 50 gives the Shabbat laws and affirms the jubilee framework: 'this entire history I have written for you.' The angel of the presence confirms that everything from creation to Sinai has been dictated from the heavenly tablets. The book ends where it began — on Mount Sinai, with YHWH and Moshe, the revelation complete.

faq.question: What is a 'week of years'?
faq.answer: Seven years. Yaakov serves Lavan 'one week of years' for Rachel (26:1). A jubilee is seven weeks of years (49 years). The entire chronological structure of Yovelim is built on this system — weeks of days, weeks of years, jubilees of years. Time itself is sabbatical.

faq.question: What does YHWH say to Yaakov before Egypt?
faq.answer: At Beersheva, YHWH appears to Yaakov in a vision of the night: 'I am YHWH the Creator of your father — I am the Creator of Avraham and the Creator of Yitzhak' (45:2-4). The descent into Egypt is within the covenant plan. YHWH identifies himself by the names of the fathers — the same covenant chain from Bereshit to Shemot.

faq.question: What is the significance of the closing verse?
faq.answer: The angel of the presence declares: 'this entire history I have written for you and the Sabbaths of the years I have reported to you' (50:2). The entire Yovelim text is framed as dictation from heaven. The closing confirms: what you have just read is not a human composition — it is from the heavenly tablets, dictated by an angel, commanded by YHWH, given to Moshe on Sinai.

faq.question: Why was Yovelim excluded from both the Rabbinical and Roman Christian canons?
faq.answer: Yovelim is one of the most well-represented texts at Qumran — 15 or more fragments across multiple caves. The Council of Yavneh (c. 90 CE) excluded it. The Council of Nicaea (325 CE) excluded it. Both otherwise competing institutions produced identical canonical exclusions. The texts they excluded are the ones that most directly describe the covenant people's identity, their covenant framework outside Roman and Rabbinical mediation, and YHWH's judgment response to the nations that enslaved them. The Beta Israel of Ethiopia preserved Yovelim (Kufale) complete for over a thousand years — in isolation from both systems.

faq.question: What does Yovelim say about the intermarriage prohibition?
faq.answer: Chapter 30 records the law against intermarriage as written on the heavenly tablets. The prohibition is tied to the holiness of Yisra'EL as YHWH's people — not to racial superiority but to covenant identity preservation. The covenant lineage is biological. The tribal designations descend through specific genealogies. Yovelim frames the preservation of this lineage as a heavenly-tablets obligation, not a human institutional rule.
